comments.text: In joint work with Wouter, we wrote a paper on this problem (arXiv:2512.22083). We obtain a new lower bound for \(v(k)\) and uncover a connection to [304]. Summary: 1. We prove an improved lower bound\[v(k)\ge \exp(c k^2)\]for some absolute constant \(c>0\). 2. Any further improvement on the lower bound for \(v(k)\) in [293] would yield an improved upper bound for \(N(b-1,b)\) in [304]. Conversely, any improved upper bound for \(N(b-1,b)\) in [304] could likely be fed back into our method to produce a better lower bound for \(v(k)\) in [293]. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

A small remark on the upper bound for $v(k)$. The usual estimate $v(k) ≤ k c_0^{2^k}$ can be slightly sharpened to \( v(k) \le c_0^{2^k} +\frac{1}{2}\), where $c_0 = 1.26408\ldots$ is Vardi’s constant. Let \((s_j)_{j\ge1}\) be Sylvester's sequence defined by \(s_1=2,\ s_{j+1}=s_j(s_j-1)+1\). Now take any representation \( 1 = \frac{1}{n_1} + \dots + \frac{1}{n_k}\), \(1 \le n_1 < \dots < n_k\), and set \(q := \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} 1/n_i\). It is well known that Sylvester's sequence gives the best $n$-term Egyptian underapproximation of $1$ for every positive integer $n$ (see, for example, [Soundararajan]; see also [Nathanson] and [Chu] for further generalisations), hence \(q=\sum_{i=1}^{k-1} \frac{1}{n_i} \le \sum_{j=1}^{k-1} \frac{1}{s_j} = 1 - \frac{1}{s_{k}-1}\). Thus \( \frac{1}{n_k} = 1 - q \ge \frac{1}{s_{k}-1}\), so \(n_k \le s_{k}-1\). In particular every denominator occurring in a $k$-term representation of $1$ is at most \(s_{k}-1\), and therefore\( v(k) \le (s_{k}-1) + 1=s_{k}\). Since \(s_k = \lfloor c_0^{2^k} +\frac{1}{2} \rfloor\), this yields \(v(k) \le c_0^{2^k} +\frac{1}{2}\).

In fact, $$v(k) \le kF(k) + 2 \le c_0^{(\frac{1}{5} + o(1))2^k}$$with $F(k)$ defined as in #148, by the upper bound in [ElPl21] mentioned there. Unrelated small remark on the definition of $v(k)$: we are interested in the smallest integer larger than 1 that does not appear as some $n_i$. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
